texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas-solvent contactors have received much attention for CO2 absorption, as the approach incorporates advantages from both solvent absorption and membrane gas separation. This study reports on pilot plant trials of three membrane contactors for the separation of CO2 from flue gas. The contactors were porous polypropylene (PP), porous polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), and non-porous polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), with the solvent PuraTreatTM F TM . To enable performance comparison, laboratory measurements based on a gas mixture of 10% CO2 in N2 were also undertaken on the same contactor-solvent systems. It was found that the PP contactor experienced significant pore wetting in both laboratory and pilot plant studies. In contrast, the PTFE contactor experienced only minor pore wetting in the laboratory. However, in the pilot plant trial of the PTFE contactor extensive pore wetting was observed, and the overall mass transfer coefficient measured was comparable with the PP contactor. The non-porous PDMS contactor had an overall mass transfer coefficient two orders of magnitude less than the PP contactor, due to the greater mass transfer resistance of the polymeric film. However, the non-porous membrane does not experience pore wetting, which resulted in the overall mass transfer coefficient being similar for both laboratory and pilot plant measurements.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: membrane-solvent absorption, also known as membrane gas absorption, is a novel approach designed to exploit the advantages of each separation technology (2). Membrane gas absorption involves the transfer of CO2 from the flue gas through a non-selective porous hollow fiber membrane contactor where it is chemically absorbed into a solvent. This approach combines the highly CO2-selective solvent with the benefits of membrane module design (3). A membrane contactor can achieve much greater mass transfer area per unit volume than conventional solvent absorption, with a 65 -75% reduction in weight and size of the contacting equipment (4). Furthermore, the membrane physically separates the liquid and gas flows, preventing foaming and reducing liquid channelling, two major issues with solvent absorption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Arange of porous contactors and solvent combinations have been trialled for CO2 removal (2, 5 -19). In most of these studies an overriding concern has been the problem of membrane pore wetting and the consequent reduction in overall mass transfer coefficient because of the difference in CO2 diffusivity between a solvent filled pore and a gas filled pore (7). To overcome this issue, a hydrophobic membrane material of very small pore size is desirable. The most common membrane materials used for their hydrophobicity are polypropylene (PP), polyethylene (PE), and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE). Others use a more hydrophilic material, but reduce pore sizes to nanometer level (20, 21). The choice of solvent is also a critical parameter in minimizing pore wetting. Solvents such as amines (mostly MEA), amino acid salts, and NaOH have all been tested. However, Kosaraju et al. (22) argue that volatile solvents such as MEA increase the propensity for pore wetting, due to sorption of MEA vapor onto the pore walls, which reduces their hydrophobicity. These authors show that the use of a non-volatile solvent, a polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer solution, is able to lead to stable performance for 55 days in a standard PP contactor.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Non-porous contactors overcome the issues of membrane pore wetting by providing a continuous polymer barrier to liquid flow, although this layer can itself result in a greater mass transfer resistance if it is too thick. Non-porous contactors tested for CO2 removal have been based on a silicone rubber membrane with DEA solution (5), or silicone rubber and polyethersulfone with water and NaOH solution (25). Kosaraju et al. (22) achieved stable performance with porous hollow fibers of polymethylpentene (PMP) having an ultrathin integral nonporous skin of high permeability, while Chabanon et al. coated a dense skin layer of thickness 1-3 µ mmade from PMP or Teflon-AF onto the liquid-facing side of a microporous PP fiber (26). Most recently, Porogen and GTI have coated a thin (0.1 µ m) layer of a superhydrophobic material onto porous fibers of poly(ether ether ketone) (PEEK) for CO2 removal from both syngas and post combustion flue gases (20, 21, 27). This approach has been successfully tested in a field trial unit with a 4-inch diameter module at a coal fired power station and will shortly be scaled up to a I MW equivalent pilot plant scale (27).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here, pilot plant trials of porous PP, porous PTFE and nonporous PDMS contactors for CO2 separation from a brown coal-fired power station flue gas are reported. The trials were conducted as part of the CO2CRC H3 capture project (28), a post-combustion CO2 capture trial. The solvent used was PuraTreat TM F, a commercial solvent marketed by BASF that has been applied to CO2 separation in traditional solvent absorption (29). In support of the pilot plant trials, laboratory measurements of CO2 separation using PP, PTFE, and PDMS contactors with PuraTreat TM F from a N2-CO2 gas mixture are also reported.
texts.text: Here, pilot plant trials of porous PP, porous PTFE and nonporous PDMS contactors for CO2 separation from a brown coal-fired power station flue gas are reported. The trials were conducted as part of the CO2CRC H3 capture project (28), a post-combustion CO2 capture trial. The solvent used was PuraTreat TM F, a commercial solvent marketed by BASF that has been applied to CO2 separation in traditional solvent absorption (29). In support of the pilot plant trials, laboratory measurements of CO2 separation using PP, PTFE, and PDMS contactors with PuraTreat TM F from a N2-CO2 gas mixture are also reported.

CLM represents the log mean average of the concentration driving force between the bulk gas phase and the liquid. As the reaction of CO2 with the solvent is rapid, the equilibrium concentration of CO2 that would be in equilibrium with the bulk can be assumed to be zero. This means that the log mean driving force can be approximated simply by the log mean average of the inlet and outlet concentrations of CO2 in the bulk gas phase (2).

texts.text: EXPERIMENTAL

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The PP contactor used at laboratory scale was a LiquiCel MiniModule (Membrana; Charlotte, North Carolina, USA) of 7400 fibers, which operates in counter-current parallel flow. The module used at pilot scale was a larger Liquicel ExtraFlow module (Membrana; Charlotte, North Carolina, USA) with 30,000 fibers. This unit includes a central baffle so that flow is no longer counter-current, but can be described as transverse flow (36). The PTFE contactor for both laboratory and pilot plant studies was an in-house built counter-current parallel flow module using PTFE fibers obtained from Markel Corporation (Plymouth Meeting, Pennsylvania, USA) (24). The non-porous PDMS contactor for both laboratory and pilot plant studies was a PermSelect PDMSXA (MedArray; Ann Arbor, Michigan, USA) of 9600 fibers, operated in counter-current parallel flow. Additional details on contactor modules are provided in Table 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The laboratory measurements were undertaken on a membrane contactor apparatus previously described (24), with a 10% CO2 in N2 gas mixture (BOC Gas Ltd) used as feed. The solvent was stored in a 20 L tank and pumped through the solvent circuit, on either the lumen and shell side of the membrane contactors for porous contactors, and only the shell side for the non-porous PDMS contactor. Solvent flowrate was controlled through pump speed, while the differential pressure across the module was controlled manually on the solvent side by a needle valve. Gas flowrate and pressure was controlled through a rotameter and back pressure regulator, respectively. Gas composition was measured with an infrared CO2 analyzer (Horiba VA3000), while solvent sampling was achieved by collecting ∼ 10 mL of loaded solvent with analysis through standard methodology (37).

texts.orig: The pilot plant studies where undertaken on a custom built plant as part of the CO2CRC H3 Capture project (28), using flue gas from a Victorian Brown Coal fired Power Station (Figs. 1 and 2). The flue gas was taken after the blower discharge of a direct contact cooler. The average feed gas composition is provided in Table 3, though the composition varied considerably between days, dependent on the operation of the power station. The flue gas pressure was increased further to ∼ 150 kPa absolute by a feed booster and subsequently cooled through a plate heat exchanger to ambient conditions. A separator vessel removed any condensed water and the flue gas was then reheated to ∼ 45 ◦ C through trace heating around the pipework. To control the feed gas pressure, a control valve on the feed line was used to divert a proportion to a bypass line. The flue gas pressure was increased further to ∼ 150 kPa absolute by a feed booster and subsequently cooled through a plate heat exchanger to ambient conditions. A separator vessel removed any condensed water and the flue gas was then reheated to ∼ 45 ◦ C through trace heating around the pipework. To control the feed gas pressure, a control valve on the feed line was used to divert a proportion to a bypass line. texts.orig: The overall mass transfer coefficients are compared with those from the pilot plant trials using the larger, crossflow PP contactor in Fig. 4. The pilot plant contactor performance provides mass transfer coefficients similar to that observed in the laboratory for low Reynolds numbers, and there is a clear trend of decreasing overall mass transfer coefficient with decreasing Reynolds number. The difference in Reynolds numbers reflects the different liquid flowrates in the two scenarios and the different flow regimes in the centrally baffled contactor used in the pilot trial relative to the countercurrent flow in the lab scale unit. The similarity in overall mass transfer coefficient indicates that pore wetting was again an issue in these larger plant trials. The liquid to gas ratio influences the residence times of both phases within the contactor, hence the increase in solvent loadings as a function of Reynolds Number (Fig. 5).

texts.orig: The overall mass transfer coefficients for the PTFE contactor are shown in Fig. 7. The mass transfer coefficients recorded in the laboratory are a factor of four greater than those observed in the PP contactor system (Fig. 3), regardless of whether the solvent is lumen or shell side. This suggests that the PTFE membrane pores are not wetted to the same extent as in the PP contactor, consistent with the prior literature. Indeed, the overall mass transfer coefficients observed here correspond well with those reported in the literature, such as a previous study by the authors for a PTFE contactor with a 30 wt% MEA solution (24) and that of Yeon et al. (38) with both PVDF and PTFE contactors and MEA. Conversely, they are greater than that reported by Kim and Yang (39) for a PTFE contactor with 2-amino-2-methyl propanol.

texts.orig: To compare these results more extensively, the pore wetting fraction was estimated for all experiments based upon Eqs. (2) to (16). It should be noted that this is a qualitative comparison only, as the properties of the solvent, and in particular the enhancement factor, are unknown and are estimated only from an equivalent solution of potassium glycinate. The calculated pore wetting fractions are provided in Table 5. These numbers suggest that under controlled laboratory conditions pore wetting of a PTFE contactor can be minimal, due to its high hydrophobicity. The difference in pore wetting between shell and lumen side is associated with the greater pressure drop on the lumen side compared to the shell. This has the effect of reducing the differential pressure between the shell and lumen sides, which increases the likelihood of solvent breakthrough. Conversely, at pilot scale both PP and PTFE membrane contactors exhibit extensive pore wetting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The reason for the extensive pore wetting observed in the pilot plant trials is partly due to the difficulty in controlling the membrane pressure differential between the solvent and gas phases. The solvent must remain at a higher pressure than the gas phase to prevent bubbling, but not be too great as to pass the breakthrough pressure leading to solvent weeping into the gas phase. This pressure differential varies across the contactor length due to the pressure drop of each phase. The gas phase in the lumen side experiences a greater pressure drop, because of the small fiber diameter, relative to the solvent phase in the shell side, which has a wider relative diameter. Hence, the pressure differential at the solvent entry into the contactor is greater than the pressure differential at the solvent exit, as shown in Fig. 8. This means the solvent inlet presents conditions that are most favorable for pore wetting, especially if there is a transient pressure disturbance, as seen in Fig. 8. Such pressure differential fluctuations are difficult to control on a fast enough timescale to prevent wetting. Similar conclusions were drawn from the Kvaerner trials of this technology (23) and our own prior work (24). This pressure differential control issue must be overcome before porous membrane contactors can be commercialized for large scale post-combustion capture.

texts.orig: The overall mass transfer coefficients for the non-porous PDMS contactor are shown in Fig. 9. The laboratory mass transfer coefficients are two orders of magnitude less than those observed in the PP contactor system (Fig. 3) and three orders of magnitude lower than those observed for the PTFE contactor system (Fig. 7). This reflects the mass transfer resistance of the thick (55 µ m) non-porous PDMS membrane layer itself. CO2 must diffuse through this dense polymeric film, rather than a gas or solvent filled pore, where diffusivity is greater. Similar to porous contactor systems, the overall mass transfer increases with the Reynolds number, because the higher solvent flowrate reduces the boundary layer resistance. Based on Eqs. (2-16), the CO2 permeability through the PDMS layer was determined to be an average of 2110 Barrers. This is less than that reported in the literature for the permeability of pure CO2, but comparable to that observed for CO2 within a wet feed gas (40).

texts.orig: little difference between the pilot plant and laboratory measurements for the Reynolds numbers studied. This is because unlike the porous contactors, pore wetting does not occur for nonporous contactors. Hence, the pressure differential across the membrane is not an issue in the non-porous contactor system and the membrane mass transfer resistance is constant irrespective of the testing environment. In this situation, the best mechanism to increase the overall mass transfer coefficient is to reduce the membrane thickness. Thus for example, Kosaraju et al. (22) achieve overall mass transfer coefficients two orders of magnitude greater than those in Fig. 9 through the use of an 'ultrathin' PMP layer, although the thickness of this layer is not disclosed. Fu et al. (41) have achieved non-porous PDMS coatings on a polyacrylonitrile substrate with CO2 permeance of up to 2000 GPU, corresponding to a thickness of only 1.6 µ m, while Porogen claim a coating of a superhydrophobic material of only 0.1 µ m. (21) Such materials would result in a membrane mass transfer coefficient comparable or less than that achieved with the porous contactors.

texts.orig: CONCLUSIONS
texts.text: CONCLUSIONS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pilot plant trials of porous and non-porous membrane contactors for post-combustion CO2 separation have been undertaken, using porous PP, porous PTFE, and non-porous PDMS contactors with the commercial solvent PuraTreat TM F (BASF). In support of the pilot plant trials, laboratory measurements were undertaken on the same contactor-solvent systems using a controlled CO2 / N2 gas mixture. The laboratory results demonstrated that the PP contactor experienced significant pore wetting, irrespective of the solvent flow regime. In contrast, the PTFE contactor had minor pore wetting because of increased hydrophobicity of the polymer. The highest overall mass transfer coefficients were observed when the solvent was shell side. In the pilot plant trials, both PP and PTFE contactors experienced significant pore wetting. This was attributed to the difficulty in controlling the membrane pressure differential, due to fluctuating pressure conditions in the pilot plant environment. This is consistent with previous pilot plant membrane porous contactor studies, and highlights the importance of membrane pressure differential control in porous contactors for industrial applications.
